perturbation.original_text: 16.1 Compliance with Laws a) Each Party shall, in connection with its obligations, rights and duties under this Agreement and in Manufacturing, handling, storage, loading, shipping, using, commercializing, reselling and distributing the Product: (i) comply with all Applicable Law or other requirements applicable to such Party's business; and (ii) subject to Subsection b) below, obtain and maintain in full force and effect all applicable licenses, permits, certificates, authorizations or approvals from local governmental authorities necessary to conduct its business and the activities contemplated under this Agreement. Such licences or certificates are to be provided to the other Party on request.
perturbation.changed_text:
19.7 Further Assurances
Each Party shall, in connection with its obligations, rights and duties under this Agreement and in Manufacturing, handling, storage, loading, shipping, using, commercializing, reselling and distributing the Product: (i) comply with all Applicable Law or other requirements applicable to such Party's business; and (ii) subject to Subsection b) below, obtain and maintain in full force and effect all applicable licenses, permits, certificates, authorizations or approvals from local governmental authorities necessary to conduct its business and the activities contemplated under this Agreement. Such licences or certificates are to be provided to the other Party on request.
The Parties shall both execute and deliver such further instruments and do such further acts as may be required to implement the intent of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the compliance with laws clause to 'Further Assurances' obscures its importance and required adherence to regulations, making it less prominent and easily overlooked. The original section on Compliance with Laws is designed to ensure all parties are aware of their responsibilities under applicable laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Moving the 'Compliance with Laws' clause to a non-standard section weakens its emphasis, potentially conflicting with 21 CFR § 210.1(a), which mandates that methods used in, and the facilities and controls used for, the manufacture, processing, packing, or holding of a drug conform to current good manufacturing practice to assure that such drug meets the requirements of the act as to safety and has the identity and strength and meets the quality and purity characteristics that it purports or is represented to possess.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text explicitly mandates compliance with applicable laws and regulations in connection with various activities related to the product. By moving this clause to 'Further Assurances,' its importance is obscured, potentially leading to a weaker emphasis on adherence to legal requirements. This contradicts the principles outlined in 21 CFR Part 210, particularly § 210.1, which emphasizes the necessity of conforming to current good manufacturing practices to ensure drug safety, identity, strength, and quality. The structural flaw of relocating the compliance clause weakens its legally binding force, making the contract potentially non-compliant with established regulations, which the law snippet 1 supports. The new location obscures the original intent, which requires compliance with the law.

perturbation.original_text: 17.2 Recalls If either Party has grounds to recommend a Recall or otherwise receives a notification or information which might result in a Recall, the Party recommending such Recall or receiving such notification or information shall immediately notify the other Party in writing. Subject to Applicable Law, Customer and its designees shall have the sole responsibility to implement any Recall of the Product or any intermediate or finished product containing the Product and the sole right to make all final decisions regarding any such Recall. Supplier shall reasonably cooperate with Customer and its designees in implementing any such Recall, at Customer's expense.
perturbation.changed_text:
19.9 Injunctions
If either Party has grounds to recommend a Recall or otherwise receives a notification or information which might result in a Recall, the Party recommending such Recall or receiving such notification or information shall immediately notify the other Party in writing. Subject to Applicable Law, Customer and its designees shall have the sole responsibility to implement any Recall of the Product or any intermediate or finished product containing the Product and the sole right to make all final decisions regarding any such Recall. Supplier shall reasonably cooperate with Customer and its designees in implementing any such Recall, at Customer's expense.
Each Party agrees that the other Party may be irreparably damaged if any provision of this Agreement is not performed in accordance with its terms. Accordingly, notwithstanding Section 19.3, each Party will be entitled to apply for an injunction or injunctions to prevent breaches of any of the provisions of this Agreement by the other Party, without showing or proving any actual or threatened damage, notwithstanding any rule of law or equity to the contrary, and may specifically enforce such provisions by an action instituted in a court having jurisdiction. These specific remedies are in addition to any other remedy to which the Parties may be entitled at law or in equity.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the recall procedure to the section on injunctions could diminish its visibility. The original location in Article 17, dedicated to recalls, ensures that these crucial procedures are readily accessible in the event of a safety concern.
perturbation.law_explanation: Placing the recall responsibilities under 'Injunctions' obscures the clear guidelines required by 21 CFR § 7.41, which details the elements that a recall communication should contain, such as identification of the product, reason for recall, and instructions for handling the recalled product. This obfuscation undermines the regulation's intent to ensure clear and effective recall communications.

perturbation.justification: The modification contradicts legal requirements by placing recall responsibilities, which should be clear and easily accessible, under a section about 'Injunctions'. This obfuscates the recall procedure, undermining the clarity required by regulations like 21 CFR Part 7, which outlines specific elements a recall communication should contain. As Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows, 21 CFR Part 7 Subpart C is specifically about 'Recalls (Including Product Corrections)—Guidance on Policy, Procedures, and Industry Responsibilities' and includes sections on 'Recall communications'. Moving the recall procedure weakens the clarity and accessibility mandated by these regulations.

perturbation.original_text: 13.4 No Consequential Damages and Limitation of Liability a) [* * *], IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE FOR ANY SPECIAL, CONSEQUENTIAL, PUNITIVE, INCIDENTAL OR INDIRECT DAMAGES, OR LOST PROFITS, HOWEVER CAUSED, ON ANY THEORY OF LIABILITY. THIS LIMITATION WILL APPLY EVEN IF THE OTHER PARTY HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGE. b) EXCEPT AS SET FORTH BELOW IN THIS SECTION 13.4(b), IN NO EVENT WILL SUPPLIER'S LIABILITY, [* * *], BE GREATER THAN, PER CLAIM OR SERIES OF CLAIMS ARISING FROM THE SAME CAUSE OF ACTION, [* * *]. EXCEPT AS SET FORTH BELOW IN THIS SECTION 13.4(b), [* * *], AS APPLICABLE, IN NO EVENT SHALL A PARTY'S LIABILITY, HOWEVER CAUSED AND ON ANY THEORY OF LIABILITY, BE GREATER THAN, PER CLAIM OR SERIES OF CLAIMS ARISING FROM THE SAME CAUSE OF ACTION, [* * *]. EXCEPT AS SET FORTH BELOW IN THIS SECTION 13.4(b), WITH RESPECT [* * *], IN NO EVENT SHALL A PARTY'S LIABILITY BE GREATER THAN, PER CLAIM OR SERIES OF CLAIMS ARISING FROM THE SAME CAUSE OF ACTION, [* * *]. NOTWITHSTANDING ANYTHING TO THE CONTRARY IN THIS SECTION 13.4(b), WITH RESPECT TO [* * *] IN NO EVENT SHALL SUPPLIER'S LIABILITY, HOWEVER CAUSED AND ON ANY THEORY OF LIABILITY, BE GREATER THAN, PER CLAIM OR SERIES OF CLAIMS ARISING FROM THE SAME CAUSE OF ACTION, [* * *]. [* * *]
perturbation.changed_text:
11.7 [†]
No Consequential Damages and Limitation of Liability
a) [* * *], IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE FOR ANY SPECIAL, CONSEQUENTIAL, PUNITIVE, INCIDENTAL OR INDIRECT DAMAGES, OR LOST PROFITS, HOWEVER CAUSED, ON ANY THEORY OF LIABILITY. THIS LIMITATION WILL APPLY EVEN IF THE OTHER PARTY HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGE.
b) EXCEPT AS SET FORTH BELOW IN THIS SECTION 13.4(b), IN NO EVENT WILL SUPPLIER'S LIABILITY, [* * *], BE GREATER THAN, PER CLAIM OR SERIES OF CLAIMS ARISING FROM THE SAME CAUSE OF ACTION, [* * *]. EXCEPT AS SET FORTH BELOW IN THIS SECTION 13.4(b), [* * *], AS APPLICABLE, IN NO EVENT SHALL A PARTY'S LIABILITY, HOWEVER CAUSED AND ON ANY THEORY OF LIABILITY, BE GREATER THAN, PER CLAIM OR SERIES OF CLAIMS ARISING FROM THE SAME CAUSE OF ACTION, [* * *]. perturbation.explanation: Moving the 'No Consequential Damages and Limitation of Liability' clause into Article 11, concerning Intellectual Property, buries it in a section less likely to be reviewed for general liability concerns. This obscures the clause and could make it harder for a party to be aware of liability limitations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Moving the liability clause to a section on Intellectual Property may not provide adequate notice or clarity, potentially conflicting with UCC § 2-719 which requires that limitations of liability be presented so that the parties are aware of them during the contracting process. This relocation makes it less likely that a party will be aware of these limitations, especially concerning non-IP related claims.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. (1)Subject to the provisions of subsections (2) and (3) of this section and of the preceding section on liquidation and limitation of damages, (a) the agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in this Article and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under this Article, as by limiting the buyer's remedies to return of the goods and repayment of the price or to repair and replacement of non-conforming goods or parts; and (b) resort to a remedy as provided is optional unless the remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. (2)Where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this Act. (3) Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages for injury to the person in the case of consumer goods is prima facie unconscionable but limitation of damages where the loss is commercial is not. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text moves the 'No Consequential Damages and Limitation of Liability' clause into the section concerning Intellectual Property. This creates a structural flaw that contradicts UCC § 2-719, as the law requires limitations of liability to be presented so that the parties are aware of them during the contracting process. Placing it within the IP section obscures it, making it less likely that a party will be aware of these limitations, especially concerning non-IP related claims. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'the agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in this Article and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under this Article'. While it allows for limitations, the Perturbation and Law Explanations argue that its placement buries the clause so as to obscure it, thus not providing adequate notice, which could nullify the limitation.
